title: Adjacency list and matrix

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Adjacency list and matrix; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Adjacency list and matrix
sources.claim: Adjacency lists use space proportional to vertices plus edges, while matrices use quadratic space and constant-time edge lookup.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Adjacency lists use space proportional to vertices plus edges, while matrices use quadratic space and constant-time edge lookup.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A sparse social graph uses adjacency lists rather than allocating a full V by V matrix. Your goal is to implement and compare list and matrix representations, including a two-dimensional matrix. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Stacks, queues and deques; Hash tables, sets and maps; Graph terminology and models; Two-dimensional arrays and matrices; Frequency and lookup patterns. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Adjacency lists use space proportional to vertices plus edges, while matrices use quadratic space and constant-time edge lookup. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Parallel edges, weights, ordering, mutation, and dense graphs influence the representation. The draft therefore rejects: Adjacency matrices are always more memory efficient because each edge is one Boolean.

includes: Choose representation by graph density, required operations and memory cost.
Implement and compare list and matrix representations, including a two-dimensional matrix.

In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
excludes: Parallel edges, weights, ordering, mutation, and dense graphs influence the representation.

outcomes: Implement and compare list and matrix representations, including a two-dimensional matrix.

examples.title: Adjacency list and matrix: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A sparse social graph uses adjacency lists rather than allocating a full V by V matrix.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A sparse social graph uses adjacency lists rather than allocating a full V by V matrix.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Lists favor sparse graphs; matrices allocate O(V^2) space regardless of edge count.
